title: 2026-06-16 2026/06/16 - SSC (Part 1 of 2)
displayTitle: 2026-06-16 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2026-06-16 2026/06/16 - SSC (Part 1 of 2) [Part 1]

chunks.text: Good morning, Minister and officials. Welcome to the Social Services and Community Select Committee. Thank you for coming back again this year. Look, just the normal process. If you could introduce yourself maybe for those watching, and then give us some opening comments, and then we'll move to questions from members. Yep, thank you. So look, at the table today, I have with me the Chief Executive, Amanda Miller, and also in the room I've got Oranga Tamariki leadership team to assist with answering any operational questions today.

Look, during this term as Children's Minister, it's been a real privilege to be able to go up and down the country, and to manage to visit all the Oranga Tamariki sites across the country. Sometimes, some of them I've been to multiple times. And in these visits, I've also been able to, on average, visit around three other community provider partners on the day as well. These visits have allowed me to have a real insight into what the frontline are feeling, the issues that they are facing each and every day, and just

their commitment to their job. Often they can do everything right and not get the result that they would like, and they get up the next day and they do it all over again. And that takes courage, passion, and conviction of people that really do care about the outcomes of our young people. So I just wanted to take a moment to thank them for the amazing efforts that they've made.

And I'd also like to acknowledge the caregivers, because I've also had the opportunity to meet caregivers from across the range. So whether they be Oranga Tamariki caregivers, provider caregivers, caregivers in the disability space, and also just those whanau caregivers that get up each and every day as well, those grandparents, aunties, uncles, that step up when a young person needs them the most. So it's been an absolute privilege to be able to do that in my time as the Children's Minister, and I look forward to continuing doing that.

The size of the Oranga Tamariki is too big. We can't understate how massive the job is. On average, Oranga Tamariki staff now support or interact with around 18,000 young people every day. Oranga Tamariki receives 108,000 reports of concern around the safety of a child per year. That's roughly one every five minutes, every day. Each of these needs to be investigated for abuse and neglect. Safety planning needs to be undertaken.

Families in crisis need to be worked with to ensure that they're referred to the services that can best help them. On top of this, social workers need to run family group conferences, meet family court obligations, engage in youth justice work, recruit and support caregivers, oversee adoptions, manage complex and high-risk situations involving children in immediate risk of harm. Oranga Tamariki staff also run and oversee care and protection residences, youth justice residences, community-based residential care homes, and community-based youth justice homes,

including for serious youth offenders. These are highly structured and highly demanding environments, dealing with some of the most challenging and high-risk young people in the country, where safety, rehabilitation, and accountability all must be managed at once in real time. And for the children who do enter care, the work continues well beyond placement. It involves securing safe homes, managing transitions, supporting education engagement, ensuring access to health and mental health services, connecting young people with drug and alcohol

supports where needed, and building long-term pathways so they're not left without support after trauma. This is a demanding, complex, and often emotionally draining work. It requires constant judgment in high-risk situations where the consequences of getting it wrong can be life-changing. I have absolute respect for all the social workers, residential staff, youth justice workers, caregivers, and frontline teams who show up every day.

The budget initiatives this year have been put together from the voices of the frontline, from what they've told us are their major concerns. The total 2026 budget package for Oranga Tamariki brings $212.8 million over five years, as well as $800,000 of capital funding across these areas of government priorities.

$93.5 million operating and $800,000 capital expenditure funding over four years for additional staffed care placements for the increasing numbers of children with high and complex needs that we are seeing. It will also enhance the range of care responses and intensive clinical services to prevent escalation of needs and placement breakdowns, providing better outcomes for children and families. $90.5 million over four years to respond to an increased number of reports of concern, and to implement efficiency measures that improve delivery

timeliness. $27.7 million over two years to progress the implementation of the recommendations made in the Karen Potasi Review. Funding will enable Oranga Tamariki to respond to increased demand for services, including increased reports of concern and family court applications. And $1.4 million over five years to address the implementation costs for Oranga Tamariki arising from the resolution agreement for the Stanford versus Attorney General case.

This funding will meet costs arising from the transfer of land and assets under the resolution agreement and the leaseback to Oranga Tamariki. Overall, I have reviewed my ministerial priorities in 2026 and reconfirmed the priority performance indicators that Oranga Tamariki will publicly report against.

These include the percentage of children in care who have been visited by their social worker at least once in the last eight weeks to ensure their ongoing safety and well-being. The percentage of critical, within 24 hours, or very urgent, within 48 hours, reports of concern, to be addressed within these time frames. Survey results of the Oranga Tamariki caregivers report, and proportion of complaints audited that were handled in a way that fully met Oranga Tamariki standards.

The Oranga Tamariki has published the progress against these ministerial priorities in the performance reports for quarter one, two, and three in the financial year of '25, '26. And the published results show that all but one are on track, that being around the reports of concern, which is why we have put some more funding into that space. We have seen an unprecedented increase in reports of concern during this term, and this budget has seen a number of significant investments to improve this outcome.

The Oranga Tamariki also reports on the government target for youth offending to achieve a 15% reduction in the total number of children and young people with serious and persistent offending. We achieved and surpassed this goal well in advance of the target, but we will continue with this work and improve, thanks to greater collaboration across the government agencies, and thanks to undertaking the new and meaningful pilots and programs across the country to address these issues long term.

For every child who comes to the attention of Oranga Tamariki and needs help, I expect that Oranga Tamariki can help to make their lives better. I believe that we can, and I believe that we are. I am heartened by the real and positive changes we are seeing across the agency and would now welcome any questions the members have.

Thank you very much, Minister. The first question is Helen Julian. Tena koe, Minister, and welcome to the new Chief Executive, Amanda. I want to first ask some questions about youth justice, and it was really concerning to read the Independent Children's Monitor report that reported that 50% of rangatahi Maori involved in youth justice had more than 10 reports of concern made about them when they were younger.

They note the pathway to prison from care to prison, and it would appear that Oranga Tamariki isn't doing enough to intervene early. What is in this year's budget to address this? Look, you raise a really good point. And it is distressing to see that some of them have had up to 10 reports, but that also tells you why this is a problem.

It's a generational problem. It's been going on for a very long time, long before many of us have even stepped into this place. And so we have to address both. And the budgets across the last budget and this budget, the focus is really in that prevention space as well, making sure that when a young person with high and complex needs comes through our doors, which is often one of the trigger points to being in the system longer, having worse outcomes because those high and complex needs haven't been either addressed

or the right care hasn't been put in place, and they do end up within the justice system. So that's why this investment into the high and complex needs, making sure that we're assessing their needs, because how can we say we're meeting their needs if we don't even know what their needs are? So that's what that investment is for, to stop those repeat reports of concern.

We've also started up some pilots, where the local iwi and community are taking that challenge on. Where the reports of concern go directly to community, and they realize, and we realize, that the phone was picked up for a reason. And so we make sure that those supports and services are wrapped around them with that first report of concern. And we're finding that we're getting less repeat reports of concern because that support has been put in earlier.

So those are some of the things that we've been doing. Do you believe that the Military Style Academy funding, the millions of dollars that is put into that, is the best use of taxpayer money and the best way to address these issues raised? What I do believe is we can't afford not to invest that money.

These young people, for a long time, as you've just stated in your previous question, have ended up in the youth justice system, and it's been treated like a holding pen for corrections. I'm not going to tolerate that. And the Military Style Academy is a holistic approach to helping rehabilitate these young people so that they don't end up in corrections.

So my answer to you is yes, I do believe it is a good value for the taxpayer, because we will have less young people ending up in our corrections system, and we'll have more young people that will actually have a brighter future in life. How many young people are currently in the latest Military Style Academy?

I've made it very clear, and I'll make it very clear with that first question, that we won't be answering questions around young people in the Military Style Academy. You would've seen that in the written parliamentary questions that you've had back, that their privacy and their families' privacy and wellbeing is more important than being used as a political football.

Are there still 10 in the Military Style Academy? I won't be answering- Sorry, she can't answer that question. Will I get- I won't be answering that question... next question will have to come- Supplementary? We're vetting the pilot No, no. We move on to covering. Moria Minister, I know that I've asked you some of these questions in question time in the House and through written questions. So I'd really like to just needle in a little bit more.

I've got some further questions and some supplementaries as well, Mr. Chair, to clarify exactly who is caring for kids in Oranga Tamariki Care, kids with highly complex needs, disabilities, in our system. Can you confirm that kids in Oranga Tamariki care system are being cared for by adequately trained staff?

So I can't confirm across the board for every single child within the care or who have come to the attention of Oranga Tamariki care. But what I can say is that was a huge concern for me when I first came into this role, which is why I've put such a large investment into making sure that we are building up the capacity and capability of Oranga Tamariki.

But also our community partners and providers and potential community partners and providers, giving them the skillsets that they need so that they can provide that critical care for our young people. I'm not going to sit here and say it's perfect. But what I will say is there has been a massive effort across the country, to make sure that the training and the suitability of our caregivers is the best that it can be.

Supplementary, thank you. Have you been made aware of any instances of security guards who may be trained as security guards, but not with the skills for the level of care needed for children, and especially children in Oranga Tamariki care.

Have you been made aware of any instances- Sorry, you can't make assumptions in a question. The question is, has she been made aware. No, you're saying that security guards didn't necessarily have the training. You're making assumptions. Just clarify. There's no assumption- Thank you, Mr. Chair. I will clarify.

So have you been made aware of any instances where security guards have been caring for children in Oranga Tamariki care? No, I've not been made aware of security guards being caregivers to children in Oranga Tamariki care. Thank you. I've not been made aware. Minister, have you, after question time and after- But can I just- Of course... Sorry to interrupt. But can I just say, if you have been made aware of a situation like that, I'd be very grateful if you sent an email through to my office so that we can follow that up. Because,

if I understand correctly, you're saying that security guards have been put in charge of the care of young people with no other supports in place, just a security guard. And I want to make sure that I can follow that up. So if you can- Thank you... please send through to my office any specifics.

And that's a concern that I really have about this process here. This process here might be a place where you can come and ask questions. But if you're aware of something like that, I would have expected that it would've been brought to my attention before coming here, so that I can deal with the safety of a young person, because that's my role, that's my job, that's my passion. That's why I get up each and every day, to make sure that young people are safe. And when I hear stuff where they might not be, it's actually quite upsetting for me sitting here and listening to the fact

that maybe a member has known that young people aren't safe, and it hasn't been brought to my attention. So I'd just like to just clarify, please bring these to my attention. Thank you so much, Minister. I can see that we're both really concerned about the safety of children in Aotearoa, and that I have brought this up in question time and also followed up with written questions and also reached out to your office just to be clear.

So what I would like to ask- Let's just be clear. The ones that have been followed up through written questions and questions in the House would've been followed up, and you would've had answers to them. So I'm talking about if you've got any scenarios where you know kids are currently in that situation, please just let my office know.

Thank you, Minister. So- Going to that point, did you, after the questions in the House, the written parliamentary questions, and the independent children's monitor report, have any verbal or written advice sought from your office into this?

I get a whole lot of advice in my role. If you want specific answers to that advice, you can put that in writing, and I can get that back to you. But I'm not going to be able to sit here and quote specific advice across my role as minister and across all the questions that have been asked of me as a minister. I really appreciate that you're- I'll just move on to the last one.

I've just got one more question. Thank you. Just confirming that, because it was brought to your attention multiple times, and I understand the massive workload that you have, am I to understand that you haven't sought further advice on these?

Okay. No, no, no. And can you absolutely- No, that's fine ... confirm that there are no staff who do not have the required training looking after kids in Oranga Tamariki? My expectation would be that every young person that is in the care of Oranga Tamariki either has a qualified person who is taking care of them with the skill set they need, or they are in placements in homes.

And are you aware of any instances where they aren't? Moving on. Next question, Lauren. Are you aware of any instances where they aren't? Excuse me. Sorry. Thank you. There's $20 million in this budget for young people with complex needs. Can you please explain what kind of complex needs they might be and what kind of programs you envision this budget to be spent on?

So, there's a whole lot of operational work that needs to go on to make sure that we are meeting the needs of these high and complex needs that are coming through our door. I think I'll hand over to Nicolette, who may be able to talk through the work program of what that looks like and how that money is going to make a difference in those young people's lives.

So, the specific funding in this budget is actually about increasing the capacity of our complex placements for exactly the reason that we want young people in care with caregivers who can meet those needs. So, we know our young people have much higher rates of disability, higher rates of mental health needs.

This is about upskilling. We want all caregivers to be skilled. We want a particular group of caregivers who have a higher degree of support and skill around meeting particular needs. It also funds an increase in our psychologists and therapeutic resources within Oranga Tamariki, so more practical support can be provided to the caregivers.

It builds off funding in the previous budget in terms of the integrated safe care work we're doing, which is in response to Whanaketia, the royal commission, which is about upskilling our caregivers across the board, making our care process easier and simpler for caregivers, and providing increased visibility and assurance around safety and a range of things in that space.

Thank you. Thank you. New question, Lauren. Oh, thank you. Thank you, Ms. Just looking at the categories in the budget, which is investing in children and young people, there are four sort of major categories. The first one is the statutory intervention and transition. Now, I understand most of that work is done by NGOs, community organizations, et cetera.

But there's very little visibility about how that is broken down, and therefore very difficult to assess the performance. So, just wondering if you could comment on whether there is an opportunity for OT to break down those categories a little bit more in budget, and then perhaps mention about how you measure the performance of those NGOs that are delivering for you.

So, just so I've got it right, I don't want to be talking about the wrong thing. You're talking about disabilities transitioning out of care? No, no. So, in the budget, investing in children and young people. Yeah. So, the bulk of that fund, which is over a billion dollars, is the statutory intervention. Yes. Gotcha.

And so, but it's over 1.1 billion. Mm. But it's just one line item. Yeah. And so, there's no breakdown of the subcategories. Yeah. That makes the visibility in the allocation, the budget allocation, difficult to assess. Yeah. So, I can speak a little bit to that, and then I might give Jesse, come speak to the changes that are being made in our contracting and commissioning space to make sure we're doing exactly what I've just spoken to.

Okay. And that we're getting really good outcomes for the money that's being spent on our young people out in the communities, whether it be NGO or our community partners. Because we were finding that the contracts that were existing when I first came in here, and most of them still are until we transition them over, are based on output, not outcomes. And so often we didn't actually know if these young people were getting good services, or if it's just moderate services, or if they were even receiving the service at all.

But there is a reporting standard for each contract. We have hundreds of contracts, so putting it line by line might be a bit difficult. But there may be another way that we could report it to get a better oversight of that. But I'll hand over to Jesse. Kia ora Yeah. So approximately 500 million per annum is invested in contracted services.

Okay. So through the NGO sector. And we're happy to follow up in writing about the specific breakdowns as how that's currently allocated. But roughly speaking, it's approximately 60% into statutory services- Right... and then about 40% into early intervention preventative services. As the minister said, the performance measures against all these various different service types do vary.

But we could provide you some insight in writing into primary performance measures across those contracts. And a lot of them will pertain to children having a safe place to stay. Sure. Yeah. Or the specific care and protection demands that they require. My supp was actually going to be about the outcomes- Yes... versus outputs.

Yeah. And I understand the complexity of doing that across multiple contracts. But, in terms of our role- Mm... it's very difficult to- Yeah... to assess how that is performing. So, Minister, are you saying that there will be- Yeah... some accountability and transparency over performance in the future?

Yeah. So, as I said, contracts were generally based on activities or output, rather than outcomes. So with the new framework for commissioning a- and procurement, contracts will be changing over to outcomes-based contracts, where the discussion can be had between each individual provider or service around what good outcomes are.

A- and I've learnt, even from talking to community up and down the country, that this is a concern for them, too. The way that the contracting environment has been in the past, and probably for a long time, is very, very competitive. And it's based on how many people you get through your door, not based on actually how are they when they walk out your door.

So it's just making sure that we're getting that balance right, so that the contracting system isn't based on competitiveness, it's based on the needs of the kids. Because far too long, children and whānau have had to meet the system's demands, rather than the system meeting the children's and whānau's needs.

So it's flipping that switch, and making sure that we're doing it the right way. Okay. A few supps on this, and I might s- kick off with a couple of them. So I might just read this out. Just Oranga Tamariki said that it relies extensively on iwi and Māori organizations, non-government organizations, and social service providers' delivery of a range of services to children and young people and their families. So it's iwi and Māori organizations, non-government organizations, and social service providers.

Could you just give a bit of a sense of the scale of that, the types of organizations and the geographic spread? Yep. I would actually love to introduce Darren, who has been working really hard over the last two and a half years around our Enabling Communities program, which is a real focus on what I've campaigned on and spoken to around devolution back to community, which I think is going to be a pathway to better outcomes for all our whānau and family.

And I think this is a perfect opportunity for Darren to speak to the work that has been going on, but also the work that's going to continue to grow over the next budget period. Sure. Thank you, Minister. Kia ora koutou. Just quickly, we are trialing at the moment nine prototypes. We call them prototypes because we're testing different kinds of partnerships and outcomes with different communities. It could be an iwi, it could be a collective of providers, it could be... So there are nine different types.

We've been working with them quite slowly to some degree because we're looking at a long-term transformation. So we want to understand what we need to do as an organization to be able to support the change. We want to be able to evaluate what the outcomes are and where the changes are. So it sits alongside what Jessie talked about, the way that we contract in services as well. So we've got them with iwi running across the country. Some of the prototypes are looking at taking some of our statutory roles and functions,

some that we've never done before, ever, as a ministry. So that's taken a lot of legal work, a lot of accountability, a lot of monitoring, and a lot of support for the partner as well. So we're very mindful that we don't want to transfer some of the responsibilities without them having the capability.

So we've invested a lot of money in capability over the years to be able to do that. What we have seen as well, even in commissioning, so Jessie spoke a little bit around what we're commissioning. We're also testing models for commissioning, that iwi might commission services in their own community.

So while the point of commissioning might sound like the interesting point, the reality is that we're testing what it looks like when iwi or Māori take leadership and commission what they think that they need. I think you referred, of course, to the ICM report. We would start to see that there's disparity for tamariki Māori.

We know that. But where we do good for all children, Māori outcomes improve as well. But we recognize that there are some disparities that we need to work for, and that is why we're working with the nine prototypes across the country. They identify for us some of the things that we would not have known.

So where we're looking at is how to transform our system. Is it in the way that we contract services? Is it in the way that we monitor? What can communities do, and iwi leadership do, that we can't do? So sorry for the long spiel. I'll give you a bit about it. Could you give us an example of, you said new models that have never been tested before. Can you give us a bit of a flavor of what that looks like?

Yes. I think the ICM report referred to two of them, for example. So I'll talk about one of them that's already come out public, which is with Ngati Awa and the Eastern Bay of Plenty Alliance. They're taking over sections, well, they've applied for us to give delegation of sections 15 and 17, where they will end up becoming the core point. So they will be making decisions on Amanda's behalf as the chief executive.

So it's quite critical. The neat part around that, what we've also supported and invested in, is that they have created their own hub of services, where the majority of government-funded services are coordinated by this iwi alliance. So families that come through our front door are getting access to services that they might not otherwise have.

So it's a coordinated service response that Ngati Awa is providing. So we didn't do that. We've hooked into them and enabled that. And what we're starting to see as well is that other government agents are seeing the benefit of that platform from which to grow from. And that's what we've always believed as well, that meeting the needs of children is not the responsibility of Oranga Tamariki alone.

There's education, there's health, all of those different providers. So Oranga Tamariki though is working with partners to partner lead, which is a big thing for us because we've never done that before, where they lead it. And so it's taking us a bit longer to understand how they want to do that and develop. So as a part of that example for you.

Thank you. My last supplementary on this is just around sort of the earlier point around all the outcome-based contracts. We actually had an interesting meeting yesterday as a committee with the Social Investment Agency. And just a point they made was that it was what you measure that actually feeds through to what, whether it's outcomes or outputs.

And often if you're just measuring, let's say, a truck roll, you're ultimately going to be an outputs measurement. So I'm interested in what measurement, metrics are you putting in place to inform these outcomes-based contracts? There are multiple metrics, so it's also up to the partner that we're working with as well. But one fundamental one that sits true for all of them at the moment is that the experience of the children and the family that comes through the support system.

So it's not just about ticking off the numbers of certain points. They want to know that experience because they believe that the experience will sharpen their ability to seek help earlier, to be able to access support that they otherwise would not, and that they will be able to get support. So that's one of the measures, is the satisfaction of whanau and children that are accessing the services. The other technical ones that we look at as a ministry is re-notification rates.

So for example, we know that there's a percentage of families that come through our system, like we heard about the 10 consecutive reports that have come through in the Youth Justice report. But actually, they keep coming back into the system. So what we're noticing as well with some of the pilots or the prototypes that we're testing is that there's a reduction in the volume of re-notifications. Which means to say, and I think the ICM report also kind of identified that there's disparity, but the further you go into the system, the more persistent that

disparity is. And so for us as well is that what we're seeing through the Enabling Communities prototypes is a disruption of some of those patterns. We haven't yet been able to analyze it, so at the end of this year we will be evaluating the Enabling Communities prototypes and the work that's happening across the country on those measures.

Sorry, I did say that was my last one, but I will have to ask one more. Just in terms of the experience, how do you measure that? Because that's a qualitative measure, and if you're talking about a child, they don't really have a comparator often to compare to. So how do you get a good measurement that you can actually inform decision-making and- You're right... inform whether the performance-based contracting methodology is working?

It is qualitative. It is a qualitative measure. But we measure that every year as well through our annual Te Matataki report, which is a survey we undertake with children that are in our care. So every year they feed into us. And so every year they tell us that actually their voices being heard, being recognized, and being actioned are what they would qualify as us as a system responding to the needs of young people and children.

So when I talk about how do we qualify experiences of young people and children, it's that, have they been heard? Have we noted it? And have we responded to what they have told us through those reports? It's always about outcomes though, the outputs. I'm coming to Donna. Supplementary. Yeah, I could go on asking questions about this- Yeah, if you want to, you go for it... all day. But in terms of the contracts, I note that in the questions that you supplied back to the committee, that in 2025 and 2026, there were 72

contracts awarded over a million dollars. But it says that the 2026/27 figure is not available yet because Oranga Tamariki has not yet finalized what, if any, contracts are expected to be awarded for the value of $1 million in the 26/27 financial year, which is imminent.

So I'm just aware that last year there was some delay for some of the organizations in delivery. They were concerned that this was- 2021... so they thought their funding was all being cut. There was a whole lot of drama, and then there was a realization that contracts had just been delayed, and there was some problem with that. So in the face of the challenging times we have for children in our communities, and if we put them at the center of this, my question is, why are- Do we not know this? And is there a risk that we end up in the same

situation we ended up in last time? Well, kia ora. I'm happy to answer that, and I'll hand to Jesse with some of the detail. So the short answer is that we are in a very different time than we were a couple of years ago. So our relationships with providers are in a far greater place. The engagement that Jesse and his team have done over the past 18 months has really rebuilt those relationships and rebuilt that trust.

We are in the process right now of extending a large number of contracts. So there are no providers in the situation of not knowing their situation for the next financial year. And Jesse, you might be able to talk to some of the numbers there. Yeah, very good. So yeah, as the chief executive's mentioned, we are extending large numbers of contracts.

In that process, we are looking for opportunities to relieve some administrative pressure on providers. So where possible, we are consolidating contracts. What that means is, for at least the next two weeks, there may be a couple more where actually it went from one or maybe up to three arrangements, and it's now been consolidated down into one.

Right. Which would obviously change the value and duration of the contract. We do expect to have that all done by 30 June, and we've been keeping providers abreast of our progress. So I think we're on track. But yeah, we will be able to report that following the end of the financial year once we have it fulfilled. And in saying that, I think, for a long time, that the contracts weren't really being seen to in a timely manner.

Often they would have time-limited funding. Mm. And because the process hadn't started early enough, often contracts were just rolled over because they were time-limited funding just rolled over, not really assessed to see what the future should look like or whether it should continue. This process- Yeah... has, over the last two years, has been around cleaning up that process, having a new framework for how we contract and procure contracts. But that also means involving community in that conversation along the way, to make sure that we get that right,

to make sure that their voices are heard, so that this new framework fits not only Oranga Tamariki but fits the community. So Anisa and the team have been doing a roadshow through all the communities, and there's been really good provider engagement in that process. The feedback is taken on board, and that has helped to design the framework of the new contracting and procurement process. The reason why we can't kind of renew those contracts with certainty without extending is because that new process needs to kick in, which gives that longer conversation about what contracts look

like, what their expectations of us are, and what our expectations of them are, so that we get good contracts that will get good outcomes. Supreme Court. That's good. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Minister, very interested in the feedback around the contracts. But noting the legal comment that was made, how then, or where does the buck stop in terms of any sort of legal liability?

If that's devolved to NGOs, does the buck stop there, or does it still sit with the chief executive? So the answer is it still sits with the chief executive. Okay. We can't delegate away our statutory obligations to young people. But what I would say to that is, sitting down with the leadership of these partners, they take that responsibility very seriously.

They have those conversations with myself that they understand that whilst the obligation sits with the chief executive, that they take full ownership of those decisions and any accountability that needs to happen. That's why we have to be very careful, work very diligently to make sure that they do understand whilst that obligation sits with the chief executive, there'll still be accountabilities on the leadership of whoever owns that contract too.

It won't just be, "Oh, you've made a mistake," and there's no ownership there, and it all goes to the leadership team here. There will be processes that we also follow to hold accountability to those leaders. Major Carter. Thank you. So we've heard the minister and the officials talk proudly about success partnering with Iwi Māori and Hapu, and about the influence of devolving to Iwi Hapu community through enabling communities.

Why, if you are focused on contracts with Iwi Māori, noting that Māori Tamariki make up three-quarters of Tamariki in care, have you removed one of the targets that Oranga Tamariki wasn't meeting, the measurement base, removing the assessment measurement for service contracting with Iwi Māori organizations in this budget?

The measurement was at 23% of the target. So can you tell me why that assessment has been removed? Yeah, so when they were looking at- Those assessments and what we report to. That was a process that went through quite a few people to come back with feedback to myself.

And the decision was an operational decision as well as to where we could be concentrating on the most important issues for our young people. I'll hand over to Amanda around the process and the decision and why. Thank you, Minister. I think first up, it's important to say that we are still measuring a number of these things internally through our operational measures. So it isn't a matter of them no longer being measured at all. This particular measure is quite a blunt one, and we're interested in doing some further

work on it. So it's not the end of the matter. It's really a bit of a reassessment of how we could make that a more appropriate measure in the future. I think it's really important to note, and you heard it from the minister and from Darren earlier, how important these relationships and contract arrangements are to Oranga Tamariki's ultimate goal, which is to be far less involved in the lives of children and young people. So, this really shouldn't be seen as any attempt to resile from that.

Hold. There's another one. We'll move Marama then. New question. Like you got three. Mm-hmm. It'd be great if I could have three as well. She can have six. For this- In a row. Your new question, Marama. Don't get a sustained line. Three in a row. It's actually a sup one. This one, if I may. Three in a row. You want a sup or a prompt?

Yeah, sup. Okay. Sup. In fact, there's a few sups on the number- All right... of questions everybody can hear, if I may. And so, Minister Moriena, kia ora tatou. Kia ora. The Iwi Māori organization piece, and the efforts to reinstate trust, good thing.

Keep doing that. But what I do think is interesting, partly, is that 40 years ago, if you want to measure, 40 years ago, Whānau Taiao was launched. And 40 years ago, we're still having to ask the same questions. So to my question, so page 153 of the budget also states the current measure.

So talking about the measuring piece of it, the targets are narrow, so the narrowing targets thing. So what is that? How come? What makes it narrow, and how would you improve that? If the target is narrow, what do you think you're looking for? What are you looking for?

And yeah, let's ask that one first. So I guess what we don't want to do is create some perverse consequences. What we want are long and enduring outcomes based contracts and relationships with Iwi Māori, and we're absolutely committed to that. It's a huge priority for me personally, and I've made these expectations clear to the organization. We're so far down the road here as it stands that we are beyond this particular measure anyway.

I think what we want to do is create a measure that's a bit more meaningful, so that encapsulates all of those aspects of the contracts rather than just the numbers. Yeah. So the narrow bit is about the numbers, and you want to know more than that. Is that what that's- I want to know more than that. Yes. Yeah. So what is the more? What makes up the more more?

So I guess it's-- Sorry to interrupt. Mm. It's the quality of those relationships, the outcomes of those contracts that we've already talked about today. It is the successful move closer to communities, iwi, hapū and whānau of decision-making.

Those are the things that will really shift the dial for Tamariki Māori. Yeah. Just as a matter of reference, Amanda, this has been trialed before. Integrated outcomes is not a brand new concept. And I know this and Darren knows I know this because we've done this stuff together or attempted to over the years. It might have a slight tweak in a description, but essentially integrated outcomes, outcomes result-based accountability performances, blah, blah, blah. All of this language has been trialed before.

What has not been trialed or what hasn't been tested is the resourcing that goes behind that. Mm-hmm. Yes. And then actually, really, truly, and enduringly enables community groups, whether they're Māori, Pākehā- Yeah... whatever those groups are made up of, that they are given the genuine and absolute trust, resource, and statutory support to do that job. So we can, like Sue and your team can get out of the way, but engage when you need to.

Yeah. I'd just like to hand over to Darren because a lot of that stuff you're speaking to is what we've been working on. And I think he's the best one to speak to, to give it the credit that it deserves. Thank you. And I think all the points that you raised, I will have to say, yes, we know and acknowledge that. So tena koe.

The biggest part that we've been looking at is, as I was trying to raise before, how do we build the capability of Māori and iwi and communities to be able to take on those roles? So to your point, in the past, we have oftentimes done integrated contracting without thinking about how to build the capability for long-term, sustainable change.

Yeah. And that has been the biggest change this time over here, is evaluating this along the way. And already we recognize that some of our systems that we have put in place might work for us- Mm-hmm... or sometimes doesn't work for us either- Yeah... but it's not going to work in a transformative way moving forward. So even how we see that and shift those decision-making.

So part of the big goal for us as well is did we shift not just resources and money? So Jessie talked about how we contract a half a billion dollars a year into contracted services, but how do we shift decision-making? So what is the decision-making that happens alongside that?

Do our whānau, hapū, iwi communities have the right to be able to make those decisions? And so it goes hand in hand with the delegations of authority- Yeah... which is a lot of work. And some of our people don't want to take that part. Mm. And so we have to work through along that one to say which part. The other part I just wanted to mention, too, is as you talk about, Poatuata Two.

Mm. And fundamentally, one of the critical pieces inside Poatuata Two was the belief that the state had a misunderstanding around whānau. Mm. And that while whānau may be harmful in some circumstances, this also can be the solution of how to work through that. So what we've been working on at the moment is how do we identify that leadership, and how do we work better with whānau to create safety and protection for mokopuna and tamariki?

What we recognize, and it's taken us as a ministry a long time to recognize, we alone cannot keep children safe. Yeah. And that the closer the eyes are to the child and the proximity of the child, the better, the safer children will be. Mm. And that we want to disrupt the patterns over that. We don't want to wait for 10 reports of concern.

Mm. Yep. So at which point, and what I think I see him kind of identified, and I'm sure that you would agree as well, is that oftentimes we don't have the right response for the situation. Yeah. And that sometimes in standardizing the responses we do as a ministry, they're not the right response for that particular family at that time.

Yeah. Understood. So the working at the moment with our iwi and communities is identifying some of that. The insights will hopefully inform our practice, inform what we do as a ministry- Mm... and be able to do that. So that's why the evaluations at the end of this year will be important to kind of tell us. But I agree with you around the integrated, just using language will not pick it up.

Mm-hmm. The sentiment behind it- Mm... must be devolving some of those decisions and that authority now. Thank you. Mm. Which is why I've set a clear kind of expectation around what I said previously, that for far too long, families and children have had to work around the system. Yep.

And they've had to meet the system's needs, rather than the system meeting their needs. And I've been very, very clear, the whole time that I've been in this space, that that needs to reverse, that the system needs to meet the needs of the kids. But I would reiterate again that the system isn't just Oranga Tamariki. The system is education, housing, mental health, and it needs to meet the whole of the child, not just bits and pieces.

Mm. Otherwise, you've just got a puzzle piece here, a puzzle piece there, and their needs slip through the cracks. Which is why we've been working alongside all other agencies, using the Social Investment Agency as one touch point, but not just stopping there. So we have a joint work program that we're getting underway with mental health between Oranga Tamariki, and critical care and mental health, that is just starting to get underway. We have a joint work program with the Ministry of Education- Yeah ...

about the importance of making sure that our young people who may have been excluded from school or have had barriers put in place of getting an education, that the focus and the priority is getting them back in front of a classroom. And that may not necessarily be a one-size-fits-all approach to how education needs to be. Mm.

Which is another critical point- Mm ... working with community, because they may be able to offer an alternative solution that meets the needs of that young person. We have a work program with transitional housing for when we have young people who have disabilities who are transitioning out of care.

Often that transition planning and process was left to the last minute, which left that young person with uncertainty and anxiety about what the future looks like. But now the planning starts a whole lot earlier, so that by the time they are 18 and they're starting to age out, whilst we still have support networks a- and provide networks to young people till they're around 21, that transition needs to be clearer, smoother, to provide clarity, to allow that young person to have the best chance of transitioning into community and having a really

positive future in life. So there's a lot of work going on, not just within Oranga Tamariki- Mm ... but within the children's system as a whole- ... which I think is going to be to the betterment of our young people. I don't think we've been as far ahead in that kind of collaboration of unbundling the silos as we are right now. But there's still- We did try with children's team too, the- Yeah... the minister you might recall.

Yeah. And also- As we expanded... you make a good point. Mm. We trial a whole lot of things. But do we actually, after that assessment- Mm... actually say, "Hey, this worked," and actually continue it as business as usual, or do those pilots or those trials get lost in all the noise?

And so that's something that we're really focused on as well. If something is working, we need to be supporting that to become what good looks like-... across the whole of the sector. Minister can I just come to- But also we need to make sure that- Sorry ... if things aren't working- Yeah ... that we're not encouraging contracts or processes that encourage people to try and continue making something that doesn't work, work So they can have the courage to come forward and say, "Can we pivot, try something new?"

So the military style academies, if I can bring it to that, ironically- Yep... following that statement. So the funding profile, Minister, it's 9.218 million. The figures sound all very, like they can nearly gloss over the reality. I'm not saying dollars don't matter. Obviously, they do matter.

But it's a consistent annual figure. Mm. And what that tells me and concerns me is, is that what you're building? Is that build it and they will come? Mm. Because if that's the consistent annual, yes, we'll just reduce prevention to this figure, and we'll put intervention there, and that blue line stretched right out here, and the little blue line for prevention stopped here- Mm... is that your policy military response to mokopuna?

A military ... You called it holistic, which I thought was the complete opposite. Military, the word in itself suggests a whole lot of other terrible and not good things, whatever word follows it. So is that your policy response, your policy program, to youth in Aotearoa and Māori youth?

Okay. So, there's a really good question around money being made available. We know that there are two areas of this that we need to look at. We know that there's prevention, so that young people don't end up in the youth justice system in the first place, which is where we are putting a lot of priorities and focuses into the prevention space. But what we also know is that there are young people that are currently in that crisis space, where we need to have that wraparound support, specialized services put in place,

which is why I called it holistic, because that is the approach that's been taken, where we, for the first time ever, have sometimes only just for the first time, asked these young people the questions that needed to be asked. And as I said in the beginning, how do we say we're meeting a young person's needs if we don't even know what their needs are?

So a lot of the process in that MSA is around actually asking the right questions, understanding the needs of those young people that haven't been met in the past, because they have in the past, maybe over multiple years, not had somebody ask those right questions. And that's how they've slipped through the crack and ended up where they are now.

And so that approach needs to be financed. We have to have certainty that we can pay the clinicians, the teachers, the activities, and things that we're providing these young people in the future so that we can have certainty that those will be available for those young people. One of- So military equals certainty? Mm.

Well, it's certainty that we are going to have the services and support that are there for young people that need it. There's no reason why, if the need at this end drops, that the funding will drop with that, because that's the process that I've been having a conversation with around contracts.

They have to be- How many have dropped? The money has to be in the right place for the right needs and the right young people and the services that they need. But you can't go and say you're going to run these programs, you need mentors, you need clinical services, you need education services, you need all these things along the way to help these young people and say, "Oh, but sorry, we're not going to put the funding aside to be able to pay for that."

The contract- Last supplementary, Ms. Helen. Thank you. I was interested in the money that went into the Patasi report, and particularly when- Sorry, can you just repeat that last one? My apologies. I was interested in the funding that was allocated to meeting the recommendations of Karen Patasi's report.

Thank you. And when you were talking just now about collective systems of responsibility, one of her recommendations is a statutory framework that defines the government agencies that are involved. I appreciate that in 2014, we thought we'd done this, but clearly, we've failed to do this.

We haven't really got a system that is a children's responsibility system. I want to know, is that a priority to revisit that and look at that particular recommendation? Because the system that is present for children is failing so badly. Look, whilst I agree that the system has failed some children, and we often talked about the system like it's an ultimate failure for all the children across the country. I just want to make it perfectly clear that for many children in this country, our workers are out there

working very hard and produce some pretty phenomenal care for our young people, and they have good outcomes. But for the ones that have not had those good outcomes, I absolutely agree. It's been a priority for myself since coming into this space, to bring government agencies together, to bring them around the table, outside of those silos of where they're making individual decision-making, and that it is a children's system as a whole, rather than it being just pepper potted around government agencies. It is a priority.

It is something that is currently, decisions are being made in that space still. And it is something where consultation still has to happen with community, with government agencies, and with all those who kind of have a role to play in what that looks like. So I won't be making any guarantees of decision-making before that consultation process has happened.

New question, Willow-Jean Thank you. Coming back to youth justice, I want to ask the minister some questions about other youth justice facilities. And in particular, what is in the budget to improve the situation at Korowai Manaaki? In April this year, we had another rooftop incident. I understand in my last written questions that there were 26 unfilled vacancies, 15 of them youth workers and one psychologist. That secure care extensions are still occurring illegally, and that a reset plan was done and completed last year, but it obviously hasn't fixed the

problems. Is the minister satisfied with the management of Korowai Manaaki? Absolutely. Because if that member wants to say that the previous government had this perfect little system of youth justice that never did anything wrong, then I'm sure she'd be the last one to be throwing the stones that she's throwing right now. But right now, this has had so much improvement to the point where staff are now telling me that they feel supported in their roles. They feel like they've been given the tools that they need to provide what those young person

needs. Occasionally, there will be something that goes wrong, like a rooftop incident. But what I can say to you, we've gone from around 15 incidences a year to two in the whole time that I've been minister. So for you to throw that out like I'm harming and hurting these kids intentionally, is actually offensive. And I am proud of the work that the youth justice team has done to improve themselves.

Not only improve the safety of the young people in their care, but improve the safety of the staff. So we're not setting the staff up to fail, and we're not setting the young people up for corrections. So yes, I'm absolutely proud of the work they've done. Not saying it's perfect, not saying that some things don't go wrong. But I'm proud of the hard work and effort that the youth justice team has put in place.

And I'm really proud of the leadership team that has taken on some of the toughest problems that OT has to face, and that has been ignored for far too long. So I will hand over here. I just wanted to know what your- No, because you asked the question No, I asked what your-- My question was, are you satisfied?

We're going to give you the answer. Wait, she's addressed- She's addressed... Sorry. Yeah, thank you, Chair. My question was- No, she's the Chair... if the minister is satisfied. I don't need to hear from the staff. Hold on. But my question was directly to the minister. I will. I was-- I was going to come to you. Yeah. Thank you. You can ask a supplementary. I have another supplementary. No, you're not. I'm the Chair. You are the Chair. Well, my question was to the minister. She's addressed my question- Yeah. And I am wanting a full answer

... she asked to hear from the staff. Because there were some terrible accusations that were made within that question Don't disrespect the Chair. Supplementary. Well, just I'll hear from her first, then the member. We want to clarify because that question had a whole lot of loaded assumptions- Mm-hmm... that need to be addressed. Now, if you want them addressed, fair enough. But if you want to just chuck out loaded questions like that and not get an answer back- Then you're not paying attention... then what are we doing here?

Well, let's hear- I have since she got the- Let's hear from her first, and then you'll come to you Point of order, Mr. Chair. Mr. Chair. Point of order. Mr. Chair, do we have to go into private session for a moment? Well, up to you. Oh, there's so many of you here, sorry. If we could please go into private session.

As long as it doesn't take up our extra time. It won't take up the time. Okay, we won't do it. Can I just- Can I just note that we have- I want to hear the answer, please... over 50- I want to hear the answer... again, lots of primary stats, Mr. Chair. Sorry. Point of order, Mr. Chair. Point of order, Willow-Jean. The minister is claiming that I've made a whole lot of wild accusations here. They are actually responses to written questions.

The unfilled vacancies, the secure care extensions that are illegal, are all answers that have been provided to my written questions. So there's nothing incorrect about these. Yep. And we're wanting to give you an answer. So do you want the answer? She gave me an answer. I'm telling you. No, we're wanting to give you a detailed answer. I'll just rule on the point of order. Look, acknowledge that, like I've said before, I want to hear from this gentleman, and then we'll come to your further supplementary.

Well, that was your sup. Okay. Thank you. Yes, go on, Mark. Thank you, Chair. I think the minister raises some very good points in answer to the question, but hopefully with a little bit more detail, and I'll try and go through your answers. Quickly, please. Thank you. Hopefully. Thank you, Chair. No, Willow-Jean. Excuse me. I'll take the answer- Respect, please... and I'll take the question. So, when it comes to- Questions are through the chair, Willow-Jean, as you well know.

Thank you, Chair. When it comes to your question around recruitment, and you can include in that retention. Obviously, we work in a very challenging environment. We work with some of our most at-risk and complex youth. And we're not immune to the same recruitment and retention issues that a lot of organizations are seeing right now.

It's a really difficult role that we're asking people to play in those organizations and in the residences. In regards to secure care, the settings are in legislation. Anytime we use secure care, it's for the safety of the rangatahi, for the safety of other rangatahi that might be around them, and for the safety of our kaimahi.

Every time secure care is used, the settings are reviewed constantly. And if those settings change, then the person is released from secure care. Every time secure care is used, it's reviewed by a senior manager, and the circumstances and the challenges around that. When it comes to the extensions, we're actually pretty happy to say that we have reduced those by a percentile from 5% to 4% over the last 12 months.

None of those situations are ideal, but we're training our staff always. And they're responding to the training by reducing the need. The overall use of secure care has reduced quite considerably over the last 12 months. And I don't think that's a reflection of the use of secure care, it's a reflection of how to manage the incidents that might lead to the need for secure care, and it's a really good reflection of the training and the work that the kaimahi has done When it comes to Korowai Manaaki itself, it is a challenging residence because of the cohort that they have.

They do take on some very high-risk young people. The more recent rooftop incident, which was attended by myself and the minister, and our chief executive, having had a lot of experience with these types of incidences in other organizations myself, I would say it's one of the best run ones I've seen.

We worked very closely with the police and fire and emergency. There was never a circumstance where the young people were going to be able to abscond. We worked with them, they were safe, and they came down. I don't think, given the challenging environment that we work in, that we could ever give any assurance that that would never happen again.

Whenever something happens, then we work on the environment, the infrastructure, the people, the training, and the context around it. We review it, we look at it, and we learn from that. And hence, we've had the significant reduction in those type of incidents that we have had from about six one year to two the next, to zero the next, and only one in the last 12 months.

I'm very, very proud to be leading the group that has made the difference that they have made, and particularly in Korowai Manaaki, given the reset that they did post-Christmas. Supplementary. Supplementary. To the minister. Is the minister aware that some Oranga Tamariki officials were warned of a possible rooftop event several weeks before the event happened in April?

No. You were not aware? I answered the question. Okay. Just confirming, you're saying you're not aware that staff were warned of a possible rooftop incident before the event occurred in April? No. That's a really strange question. I'm not sure.

Well, not really. What is the cost of a rooftop incident? I'm not sure that leaving it unrepaired would be the best thing for those young people. Do you know what the cost was? So spending the money is important around the safety of the young children. I don't know what you're trying to establish here. So you don't know what the cost is?

I, my question was- Supplementary, Laura. Supplementary, Laura. Can I suggest, would you say it's good use of tax fund or taxpayers' money to be paying for KFC to get young people off the roof as an alternative? Absolutely not. Or what about prevention, so they don't get up on the roof in the first place? No. Okay. New question, Jamie. Oh, sorry. Final question. New question, Jamie. Thank you, Minister.

In your opening address, you discussed about responding to reports of concern. And just sitting here, I think you said one in every five minutes of concern. Yeah. If you took that across the year. Yeah. And obviously that's over a day, when it's open, those reports will be flooding in.

As a New Zealander, just to hear those numbers are quite shocking. Mm. So I'm just wondering, firstly, what does the ministry understand by those reports of concern? And obviously, in the last couple years- Mm... there's been a lot more concern reports coming in. Yeah. And so, there's lots of different ways we can look at this, right?

So reports of concern don't necessarily mean that the young person is at immediate risk of harm. Sometimes someone will pick up a phone because they have a concern that maybe this family needs some help. It could be around their financial circumstances.

It could be around absolutely anything. But this shows us, with around 105,000 reports of concern in a year, show us that more people are actually willing to pick up the phone now, and have that conversation about caring about their neighbor, caring about a young child and their life, and actually taking the time to pick up the phone and have that conversation.

So for me, that side of it is quite positive, because a hidden problem is really hard to solve. But a problem that's out in the open and people are willing to have a conversation about, is easier to try and tackle and manage. And we've also got the other stat that kind of surprised me, around Oranga Tamariki itself, and the staff of Oranga Tamariki, actually touch point with around 18,000 children a day, and do something for around 18,000 children a day in some shape or form.

And so, that just shows the effort that is really going in to make sure that young people have their needs met. So we don't just deal with the young people in our care. We often deal a lot in that prevention space. And what people don't realize is that the majority of Oranga Tamariki's work is to prevent young people coming into care.

And I think that point is often lost. Just supplementary if I may, Mr. Chair. Mm. Sure. In the estimates in the budget- Mm... you've allocated considerable funding. It's over 19 million over the next four years. And 19.8 million in this year's budget. What response and what are you expecting to see from that investment?

Yeah. There have been a lot of reviews and reports into responses. Karen Potasi review is one of them. And one of the points that was made within that review was, that poor young child had so many different touch points along the way, and he was like the hidden child.

Because one agency had a concern raised, another one had a concern raised, but nobody actually followed through with any action of those. And it's like pieces of the puzzle, but nobody saw the whole puzzle. So part of that is the hub. And part of that is we've initiated a hub within our national call center, which is specifically dedicated to the young people who have a parent that is incarcerated, and making sure that we have sight of that young person, making sure that they are safe.

Making sure that whoever is taking care of that young person as well, which is a new approach, that we're making sure that they have the supports they need to take care of that young person until their parent comes out of the corrections system. So rather than knocking on their door in a real way of, are these children safe? If they're not, here we come.

It's more of a knocking on the door and saying, "Have you got everything that you need, to take care of this young person? What can we do to help?" And that hub helps to initiate that, because we have all the agencies around the table to make those decisions and all the information, every piece of the puzzle, is in one room.

So that's where some of the investment is going. The other part is we know that our frontline social workers have been under the pump for a very long time. It's not new. It's probably been around since I was a young kid, where social workers have talked about, now it's caseloads, and the pressure of beating their statutory obligations.

So a lot of that funding will go into support for our frontline social workers. And some of that will be in the role of the allied support workers that we trialed, to work alongside social workers to do the work that might not necessarily be at a statutory level, but will take some of that pressure and that load, so that social workers can concentrate on their statutory work.

Minister, I might just- Yeah... go. Thank you. And just a couple, if we can just sort of short, quick questions last as I've got a few questions coming. This was my primary at the beginning, and it's a very good start on this. So, I am concerned that I hear about people who are being told that there is no further action required when they're still really concerned.

Yeah. That's turned up three or four times to me. There was a report from Oranga Tamariki itself in June 2025, which suggests that one of the unintended consequences of the capacity of the system being reached is that we might get that pattern emerge. What work is being done to make sure that when you reach the capacity, you don't find people taking courses of action like that?

Are we keeping an eye on the no further action amounts as there's been an unprecedented record of reports of concern? But are we also catching the other things that that report talks about that might show a false stabilization in numbers? How are you doing it?

Okay. So, I too am concerned about the situation that you're talking about, which is why we're putting such a massive investment into making sure that the area around reports of concern, have more resourcing, have more training, and have more capacity. And looking at ways where we can build in efficiencies into that system, so that the very thing that you're talking about right now, that doesn't happen. So yes, very concerned about it, but that's the reason why all these things we've spoken about today around training, capabilities, capacity,

is so important, and why we've put that major investment into that report a concern area. Will we measure that matter of how many are being dropped out of the system and see whether it increases? That's a technical, so I'll just hand it over to Nicolette. So I'll be very brief, but there are a range of things that we're doing both to improve the way we make decisions at the front door and how we measure them.

So, we've introduced, for example, a digitized report of concern to make it easier for people to report and to get really clear information and to be able to use that information more clearly. We've introduced a range of practice tools, which we've finalized at the end of last year. We're seeing, because what we do is we look regularly at cases and do case file review to make sure exactly that we understand the trend that you're talking about.

So we've got one of those around our front door initial assessment, just about due for completion at the moment. What it's showing us is that actually there's been a significant improvement in the tools we used to make better decisions. When they're used as intended, we see less of that. But it's absolutely true that demand pressure does have an impact, and so that's why the combination of things that the minister's referred to, more allied support workers who can free up social work time to do that initial assessment work, technology improvements in

our contact center, improvements in the way that we redesign of a decision response tool, which is how we make those decisions in the first place. All of that is gearing us up, both to respond to the increase we have at the moment and looking ahead to what, not just mandatory reporting might mean, but actually mandatory training.

So we want people to identify and report, and then we want to be able to respond. Your question, Karanga. Thank you, Mr. Chair. The number of section 78 uplifts has increased from 397 in 2021/22 to 682 in '24/25. The proportion of sections 78 uplifts that are without notice has increased and now make up three quarters of the section 78 uplifts In 24/25, compared to half that in 21/22. Can you confirm that we are once again seeing a disproportionate uplift of Māori

kids in this country, as stated on page 116 of the No Further Action Oranga Tamariki report? Before I pass over to Nicolette to talk about the particular area that you're talking about, I just want to make it very clear that when I came into Oranga Tamariki, I made it clear that it wasn't a numbers game. Because for a while there, there was this crowing of a 75% drop of children coming into care like it was a success, but they couldn't tell me if those children were safe. If a young person needs intervention

and needs to be put into a safe place while we put support networks in place for whānau to be the best versions of themselves, whether it be temporary or sometimes unfortunately, it's not temporary. I am happy and I am- So can you confirm- I stand by that... that kids are safer in the system, in the Oranga Tamariki system, rather than with wider whānau?

Which, uh- Can you confirm that? Well, when a young person gets removed and put into care, a high percentage of those young people are with whānau. I think there's this misconception that when a child gets put in care, that they're in non-care. A child in care can be in whānau care, so just being careful with that, saying would they be better with whānau.

And what are your ministers doing- The majority of them are with whānau... to ensure those kids are going to whānau, rather than into the Oranga Tamariki system, where there's no guarantee they'll be safer? Everything we do is trying to prevent a young person going into care. But there are some- You cut prevention funding across the country.

No, we didn't. You did. So we are try— no, we didn't. We are concentrating on making sure that the wellbeing of the children come first. And if that means intervention, and that means that we have to step in and remove that young person for a time, I am not going to stand here and say that that's better.

So you're guaranteeing they will be safer in Oranga Tamariki care? I can't make any guarantees in life because in Oranga Tamariki care, they could be with whānau, they could be with non-care whānau. And I am not in everybody's house. What I can say is there is an expectation to make sure that when we are doing placements, that we are doing good placements, and we are making sure that those caregivers have the support they need to look after those young people.

Did you read the Independent Children's Monitor report that said that there is an increase of children being given back from carers back into the system because they don't have that support around them? Yeah. And I've also read reports, right, that have said multiple things. I've also read reports that said the highest risk that a young person is in, is when they are returned to whānau.

So we have scenarios where- Are you talking about whānau or wider whānau? Return home. Return home. Return home. Thank you for clarifying that. And so the problem is, there is always going to be a scenario where there's potential harm. So you're happy with the increase from- But you are expecting me- Let her finish. Let her finish, please... 397 uplifts to 682 uplifts?

You're expecting me to guarantee that no child is going to get hurt or harmed, and I can't guarantee that. Can you guarantee they'll be safer- I can-... once they have been uplifted without notice? I can guarantee that my expectation is that when Oranga Tamariki is intervening in a situation where a child needs to be kept safe, that my expectation is that that young person is safe.

All right. Well, thank you very much, Minister, and thank you also, chief officials, for your time this afternoon. Thank you for the work you do on behalf of some of the most vulnerable children in the country. Appreciate it. Bye.
